source_intake_record.raw_question_text:
Block A and plank B are placed on a horizontal ground. The end of a fixed smooth arc track is tangent to the plane of the upper surface of B. A vertical baffle P is fixed on the ground. A horizontal external force acting on A makes A and B move to the right at a constant velocity $v_{0}$. When the left end of B passes the end of the track, slider C, which starts sliding down from a certain position on the arc track with no initial velocity, reaches the lowest point and slides onto the upper surface of B with a horizontal velocity $v$. At the same time, the external force is removed. At this moment, the distance between the right end of B and the baffle P is $s$. Given that $v_{0}=1\mathrm{m}/\mathrm{s}$, $v=4\mathrm{m}/\mathrm{s}$, $m_{\mathrm{A}}=m_{\mathrm{C}}=1\mathrm{kg}$, $m_{\mathrm{B}}=2\mathrm{kg}$, there is no friction between A and the ground, the coefficient of kinetic friction between B and the ground is $\mu_{1}=0.1$, the coefficient of kinetic friction between C and B is $\mu_{2}=0.5$, B is long enough so that C will not slide off B. The collisions between B and P, and between A and B, are both elastic collisions, neglecting the collision time. Take the magnitude of gravitational acceleration as $g=10\mathrm{m}/\mathrm{s}^{2}$.

1. Find the height $H$ through which C slides down.
2. If B and C can reach a common velocity before colliding with P, and A and B do not collide, determine the range of $s$.
3. If $s=0.48\mathrm{m}$, calculate the work $W$ done by the frictional force on C before B collides with P.
4. If $s=0.48\mathrm{m}$, calculate the magnitude of the change in the total momentum $\Delta p$ of the three objects from the time C slides onto B until A, B, and C all reach a state of equilibrium.

issues: metadata.image_paths contains a different image path than the top-level image_paths, and only the top-level path has explicit validated file metadata in the provided fallback manifest.

image_dependency: requires_image=true and text_dominant=false indicate the sample is intended to rely on the figure

An image asset is present and validated in asset_registry_record.image_manifest
multi_step: The prompt has 4 numbered sub-questions, not a single-step ask
Solving requires combining multiple mechanics stages: sliding on track, transfer onto B, frictional interaction, possible common velocity, elastic collisions, and momentum change
The question includes many interacting parameters and conditions (v0, v, masses, two friction coefficients, elastic collisions, distance s), indicating substantial chained reasoning
verifiability: answer_present=true and the answer lists explicit numeric outputs for all 4 parts
The outputs are in checkable forms: a height, a range for s, a work value, and a momentum-change magnitude
registry_passed=true, so the core text/image/answer assets were successfully registered

A vertical baffle P is fixed on the ground.
A horizontal external force acting on A makes A and B move to the right at a constant velocity $v_{0}$.

When the left end of B passes the end of the track, slider C, which starts sliding down from a certain position on the arc track with no initial velocity, reaches the lowest point and slides onto the upper surface of B with a horizontal velocity $v$.
At the same time, the external force is removed.
At this moment, the distance between the right end of B and the baffle P is $s$.

Given that $v_{0}=1\mathrm{m}/\mathrm{s}$, $v=4\mathrm{m}/\mathrm{s}$, $m_{\mathrm{A}}=m_{\mathrm{C}}=1\mathrm{kg}$, $m_{\mathrm{B}}=2\mathrm{kg}$, there is no friction between A and the ground, the coefficient of kinetic friction between B and the ground is $\mu_{1}=0.1$, the coefficient of kinetic friction between C and B is $\mu_{2}=0.5$, B is long enough so that C will not slide off B.
The collisions between B and P, and between A and B, are both elastic collisions, neglecting the collision time.
Take the magnitude of gravitational acceleration as $g=10\mathrm{m}/\mathrm{s}^{2}$.
Find the height $H$ through which C slides down.
If B and C can reach a common velocity before colliding with P, and A and B do not collide, determine the range of $s$.
If $s=0.48\mathrm{m}$, calculate the work $W$ done by the frictional force on C before B collides with P.
If $s=0.48\mathrm{m}$, calculate the magnitude of the change in the total momentum $\Delta p$ of the three objects from the time C slides onto B until A, B, and C all reach a state of equilibrium.

agent_assessment.rationale: The problem text is detailed and self-contained, and the diagram clearly reinforces the spatial setup: A and B moving right, C descending from the arc onto B, and baffle P on the right. The expected answers are fully specified. The image is useful for geometry but is semantically readable.

risk_flags: metadata.image_paths contains a different image path than the top-level image_paths, and only the top-level path has explicit validated file metadata in the provided fallback manifest.
cleaning_records.decision_rationale: The sample is semantically usable: the question and answer are complete, the rewrite is usable, and solvability is strong. However, alignment is marked risky with consistency conflicts, and there is an image path metadata mismatch, so manual review is warranted rather than automatic pass or rejection.

open_ended_problem_variants.rewritten_question_text:
Block A and plank B are placed on a horizontal ground. The end of a fixed smooth arc track is tangent to the plane of the upper surface of B. A vertical baffle P is fixed on the ground. A horizontal external force acting on A makes A and B move to the right at a constant velocity $v_{0}$. When the left end of B passes the end of the track, slider C, which starts sliding down from a certain position on the arc track with no initial velocity, reaches the lowest point and slides onto the upper surface of B with a horizontal velocity $v$. At the same time, the external force is removed. At this moment, the distance between the right end of B and the baffle P is $s$. Given that $v_{0}=1\mathrm{m}/\mathrm{s}$, $v=4\mathrm{m}/\mathrm{s}$, $m_{\mathrm{A}}=m_{\mathrm{C}}=1\mathrm{kg}$, $m_{\mathrm{B}}=2\mathrm{kg}$, there is no friction between A and the ground, the coefficient of kinetic friction between B and the ground is $\mu_{1}=0.1$, the coefficient of kinetic friction between C and B is $\mu_{2}=0.5$, B is long enough so that C will not slide off B. The collisions between B and P, and between A and B, are both elastic collisions, neglecting the collision time. Take the magnitude of gravitational acceleration as $g=10\mathrm{m}/\mathrm{s}^{2}$.
